opp.name: RWDC Industries_S2C_ Opp May '26
opp.next_step: Book discovery with James Noone to confirm RWDC’s priorities across sourcing, contracts, supplier onboarding, and supplier governance. Map current sourcing and supplier categories to show where iSource improves event speed, supplier comparison, savings tracking, and bid control. Review contract workflows with procurement and legal to position iContract around renewals, obligations, approvals, and supplier agreement visibility. Assess supplier onboarding and data quality to align iSupplier with supplier qualification, documentation, profile accuracy, and collaboration. Run a tailored demo and value case connecting iSupplier, iSource, and iContract to procurement scale, supplier reliability, savings discipline, and contract control.
opp.ais_why:
* As the business scales, RWDC needs "stronger procurement control across sourcing, supplier collaboration, and contract governance" - a foundational control requirement.
* The driver is governance and control across sourcing, SRM and CLM, not AI capability.
* Next step is to "Book discovery with James Noone... and run a tailored demo connecting iSupplier, iSource, and iContract"; Merlin sits in scope but AI is not part of the stated need. Qualified at 10%.

sources: Next_Step field (rep-generated discovery plan, not buyer confirmation)
No Avoma transcripts available for 29 May call
Salesforce Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c fields not populated (or not present in this org)
metrics.narrative: No quantified business impact or financial metrics have been captured. The 29 May introductory call with James Noone (CPO) was not recorded in Avoma, so no buyer-articulated cost savings target, cycle-time reduction, supplier-risk mitigation value, or procurement efficiency ROI exists in the evidence base. The Next_Step field references confirming RWDC's priorities across sourcing, contracts, supplier onboarding, and governance, but this is a rep-side plan, not a buyer-stated need. No Salesforce free-text fields (Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c, Compelling_Event__c) contain quantified or qualitative pain statements. The move is to run the discovery call with James Noone and explicitly ask: what is the annual cost of your current sourcing/contract/supplier process, what procurement cycle-time or savings leakage are you solving for, and what quantified outcome would justify this investment by December 2026.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: James Noone (Decision Maker) only; no Champion role, no evidence of advocacy behavior
LastActivityDate 04 Jun, 31 days ago; no recent proactive engagement
Salesforce X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c, Champion_Identified__c fields not populated (or not present)
No Avoma evidence (29 May call not recorded)
champion.narrative: No champion is identified. James Noone (CPO, Decision Maker contact role) is the only mapped stakeholder, but no evidence of advocacy, proactive engagement, internal selling, or access to the Economic Buyer exists. The 29 May call was not recorded, so no conversational evidence of James pushing our agenda, introducing other stakeholders, or sharing internal competitive intelligence is available. Contact engagement is passive: LastActivityDate 04 Jun (31 days ago) with no follow-up call scheduled or confirmed per Next_Step. Salesforce X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c and Champion_Identified__c fields are either unpopulated or not present. A true champion would be driving the process, opening doors to finance/legal/IT, and actively selling Zycus internally; James Noone shows none of that behavior in the evidence. The move is to test James's willingness to champion on the discovery call by asking him to introduce the Economic Buyer, share RWDC's internal evaluation timeline, and confirm whether he will advocate for Zycus in competitive evaluations. If he resists or defers, seek a second contact who will.

sources: Salesforce Competitor field: SAP Ariba
Salesforce How_are_you_addressing_your_problem_toda__c, Existing_vendor__c, Replacing_What__c, Why_not_Zycus__c, Zycus_Differentiation_Why_Zycus__c fields not populated (or not present)
No Avoma evidence (29 May call not recorded)
competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is named as the competitor in the Salesforce Competitor field, but no detail on Ariba's role (incumbent vs shortlist peer), pricing, buyer sentiment, or competitive positioning exists. The Next_Step references 'map current sourcing and supplier categories' and 'run a tailored demo,' but no evidence shows how Zycus is differentiated against Ariba in RWDC's eyes. Salesforce fields How_are_you_addressing_your_problem_toda__c, Existing_vendor__c, Replacing_What__c, Why_not_Zycus__c, and Zycus_Differentiation_Why_Zycus__c are either unpopulated or not present. The 29 May call was not recorded, so no conversational evidence of why RWDC is evaluating Zycus alongside or instead of Ariba, what Ariba does well or poorly for them, or what would make Zycus win exists. The wedge is unknown. The move is to ask James Noone on the discovery call: is Ariba your incumbent or a shortlist peer, what does Ariba do well and where does it fall short for RWDC, and what would Zycus need to prove to win this evaluation. Then position Merlin (in scope per Products field) as the AI negotiation and supplier-intelligence wedge that Ariba cannot match.

sources: Next_Step field (rep-inferred pain areas, not buyer-stated)
Salesforce Customer_Business_Problem__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c, What_if_this_is_not_done__c, Compelling_Event__c fields not populated (or not present)
Competitor field lists SAP Ariba but no context on incumbent vs peer status
No Avoma evidence (29 May call not recorded)
identify_pain.narrative: No specific operational or financial pain has been articulated by the buyer. The Next_Step field implies inferred pain areas (sourcing event speed, supplier comparison, contract renewals/obligations, supplier qualification, profile accuracy), but these are Zycus's product-capability assumptions, not buyer-confirmed pain points. The 29 May introductory call with James Noone was not recorded, so no direct buyer quote on what is broken today, what manual workarounds they suffer, or what cost/risk they are trying to eliminate exists. Salesforce Customer_Business_Problem__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c, What_if_this_is_not_done__c, and Compelling_Event__c fields are either unpopulated or not present. The only weak signal is that SAP Ariba is named as a competitor, which could imply RWDC is either replacing Ariba (a strong pain signal) or benchmarking Zycus against Ariba as a shortlist peer (a weaker signal), but no evidence clarifies which. The move is to use the discovery call to ask James Noone: what is not working in your current sourcing/contract/supplier process, what pain are you trying to solve by December, what happens if you do not solve it, and who owns that pain internally.

sources: Next_Step field (rep-generated plan to discuss contract workflows, not buyer-stated paper process)
Salesforce Current_Contract_Expiration__c unpopulated
No Avoma evidence (29 May call not recorded)
paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics, legal review process, procurement channel, or InfoSec requirements have been documented. No evidence exists on whether RWDC requires an MSA, SOW, or redline negotiation, whether a systems integrator or implementation partner is involved, or whether InfoSec/IT security review is a gate before contracting. Salesforce Current_Contract_Expiration__c is unpopulated, so no incumbent-contract forcing function is visible. The Next_Step references 'review contract workflows with procurement and legal' to position iContract, but this is a rep-side positioning task, not a documented buyer paper-process requirement. The 29 May call was not recorded, so no conversational evidence of legal timelines, procurement policy, or SOW scoping exists. The move is to ask James Noone on the discovery call: what is your contracting process (MSA, SOW, legal review SLA), does IT or InfoSec need to approve before signature, and are there any procurement-policy requirements (e.g. competitive bids, board approval) we should plan for.

sources: OpportunityContactRole query: only James Noone (CPO, Decision Maker) mapped; no EB or Executive Sponsor role present
Salesforce X10a_Sponsor__c, Who_will_approve_budget__c, Executive_Sponsor_Identified__c fields not populated (or not present)

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer is identified in contact roles, Salesforce EB fields, or call evidence. At $100k ACV, budget authority typically resides with CFO, VP Finance, or a senior executive sponsor above the CPO level, but no such contact appears in the opportunity contact roles (only James Noone, CPO, Decision Maker). No Salesforce fields X10a_Sponsor__c, Who_will_approve_budget__c, or Executive_Sponsor_Identified__c contain a named EB. The 29 May call was not recorded, so no conversational evidence of budget ownership, approval chain, or executive sponsorship exists. The only inference is that James Noone, as CPO, likely influences the business case but probably does not control final commercial approval at this deal size. The move is to ask James Noone directly on the next discovery call: who approves a $100k procurement platform investment, do you need CFO or executive sign-off, and can we meet that person to align the business case and timeline.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: only James Noone (Decision Maker) mapped; no committee or approval-chain visibility
Salesforce What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, Multiple_approvals__c fields not populated (or not present)

decision_process.narrative: No decision process or approval chain has been documented. The only process signal is the contact role (James Noone, Decision Maker), but no evidence clarifies whether this is a single-approver decision or a committee evaluation. Salesforce What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, Multiple_approvals__c, and Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c fields are either unpopulated or not present. The 29 May call was not recorded, so no buyer explanation of procurement governance (e.g. sourcing committee, legal review, IT security sign-off, CFO approval gate) exists. The single-thread risk (only James Noone mapped) means we have no visibility into who else participates, when they enter, or what their role is. The move is to ask James Noone on the discovery call: walk me through your decision process from now to December, who else evaluates vendors (procurement team, IT, legal, finance), are there formal approval gates, and when does each stakeholder need to be engaged.

sources: Next_Step field (rep-generated demo plan, not buyer-confirmed criteria)
No Avoma transcripts available
Salesforce Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c, AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c fields not populated (or not present)
decision_criteria.narrative: No formal evaluation criteria, RFP requirements, or buyer-stated decision framework have been captured. The Next_Step field outlines a rep-side plan to demo iSupplier, iSource, and iContract capabilities (sourcing event speed, contract renewals/obligations, supplier qualification), but this is Zycus's product positioning, not the buyer's confirmed decision criteria. No recorded discovery call transcript exists to surface what RWDC will score vendors on (e.g. ease of supplier onboarding, contract compliance automation, sourcing savings transparency, ERP integration depth, AI-driven analytics). Salesforce fields Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c, and AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c are either unpopulated or not present in this org. The risk is that the demo is built on assumptions rather than buyer-validated priorities. The move is to use the discovery call to ask James Noone: what are your top 3-5 must-have capabilities, what will you score vendors on, are there any deal-breaker gaps (e.g. ERP integration, user adoption, reporting), and what role does AI/Merlin play in your evaluation (given Merlin is in scope per Products field).

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.
blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.full: Losing on capability fit — No AIS score, status, or why-explanation is populated in Salesforce, so no formal AI-fit assessment exists. Merlin is in scope per Products field, which implies some AI appetite or at least willingness to evaluate, but no buyer-articulated AI need, use-case validation, or excitement signal has been captured. The 29 May call was not recorded, so no conversational evidence of AI hunger (e.g. 'we need AI-driven negotiation,' 'automate contract intelligence') exists. The inference is AI Curious at best (Merlin in scope but not yet validated) with risk of AI Resistant if the buyer sees Merlin as nice-to-have rather than must-have. Discovery must surface whether AI is a decision criterion or a differentiator.
bullets.text: Losing on capability fit — No AIS score, status, or why-explanation is populated in Salesforce, so no formal AI-fit assessment exists.

bullets.full: Buyer is leaning elsewhere — Merlin is in scope per Products field, which signals AI appetite or at least openness, but no evidence shows Merlin has been positioned, demoed, or landed with the buyer. The 29 May call was not recorded, so no conversational evidence of AI needs, Merlin excitement, or negotiation-agent use-case validation exists. Salesforce AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c and AIS fields are unpopulated. The Next_Step references running a tailored demo connecting iSupplier/iSource/iContract but does not explicitly call out Merlin or the agentic narrative. The risk is that Merlin is scoped but not sold. The move is to make Merlin the centerpiece of the discovery demo: position the negotiation agent on supplier price history and contract renewal optimization as the wedge against SAP Ariba.

items: Re-engage James Noone (CPO) immediately to schedule the discovery call outlined in Next_Step. (by 10 Jul)
Identify and engage the Economic Buyer (likely CFO or VP Finance at $100k ACV). (by 19 Jul)
Position Merlin AI negotiation agent as the wedge against SAP Ariba. (by 24 Jul)
blocks.title: What moves it forward

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 14 is driven by Verdict 'At Risk' — weak forward intent. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.win_position: Win 4 is carried by competitive strength +0.20 (weight 15). Offsetting it: momentum 47 vs stage-expected 50.
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 47 (roughly flat): Engagement cadence dropped (pulse cooling). Eased toward flat — 31d quiet (>30d window); momentum down 1pt (stalling).
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 1 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement). Reduced because the most recent contact is stale (recency ×0.54).
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 0 is anchored on the stage win (4), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 47, and dragged by risk 14. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

The inference is AI Curious at best (Merlin in scope but not yet validated) with risk of AI Resistant if the buyer sees Merlin as nice-to-have rather than must-have. Discovery must surface whether AI is a decision criterion or a differentiator.

champion_strength.source: OpportunityContactRole (James Noone, Decision Maker), LastActivityDate 04 Jun, no Avoma evidence of champion behavior
champion_strength.summary: No champion is identified. James Noone (CPO, Decision Maker) is the only mapped stakeholder but shows no evidence of advocacy, internal selling, or access to the Economic Buyer. Contact engagement is passive: last touch 04 Jun (31 days ago), no follow-up scheduled. A true champion would be driving the process and opening doors to finance/legal/IT; James shows none of that behavior.

items.action: Re-engage James Noone (CPO) immediately to schedule the discovery call outlined in Next_Step. On the call, surface quantified pain (annual cost of current sourcing/contract/supplier process, procurement cycle-time or savings leakage), map the Economic Buyer and approval chain, confirm decision criteria and process, clarify SAP Ariba's role (incumbent vs peer), and test James's willingness to champion by asking him to introduce finance/legal/IT stakeholders.
items.trigger: 31-day buyer silence since LastActivityDate 04 Jun; Next_Step discovery plan dated 18 Jun but no buyer-confirmed date.
items.expected_effect: Restarts two-way engagement, builds MEDDPICC baseline (pain, EB, criteria, process), tests champion strength, and unblocks progression from cooling Qualified stage. Without this, deal ghosts or slips to 2027.

items.action: Identify and engage the Economic Buyer (likely CFO or VP Finance at $100k ACV). Ask James Noone on the discovery call to introduce the budget owner. If James resists or defers, escalate to the deal owner's manager (executive connect) to reach the EB directly and align the business case, timeline, and C-level sponsorship.
items.trigger: No Economic Buyer contact role or engagement evidenced; at $100k ACV, budget authority likely sits above CPO level.
items.expected_effect: Secures budget-owner visibility and C-level sponsorship, de-risks procurement-level stall, and validates forecast defensibility. EB engagement is a gate to progression beyond Qualified.

items.action: Position Merlin AI negotiation agent as the wedge against SAP Ariba. On the discovery demo, make Merlin the centerpiece: show the negotiation agent on supplier price history and contract renewal optimization, tie it to RWDC's procurement savings and supplier-risk priorities (once confirmed in discovery), and frame it as the capability Ariba cannot match. Validate AI appetite and whether Merlin is a must-have or nice-to-have in RWDC's evaluation.
items.trigger: Merlin is in scope per Products field but no evidence shows it has been positioned or landed; SAP Ariba is the competitor but competitive wedge is unknown.
items.expected_effect: Differentiates Zycus with AI-driven negotiation and supplier intelligence, creates competitive separation from Ariba, and tests whether AI is a decision criterion or just a nice-to-have. If Merlin lands, it becomes the Phase 1 or Phase 2 win lever.

items.action: Multi-thread beyond James Noone. Ask James on the discovery call to introduce procurement team members, legal (for contract workflows), IT (for ERP integration and InfoSec), and finance (the Economic Buyer). If James does not open doors, the deal is single-thread and at high ghost risk; escalate to the deal owner's manager (executive connect) to reach additional stakeholders directly.
items.trigger: Single-thread risk: only James Noone (CPO, Decision Maker) mapped; no multi-stakeholder committee, Champion, or EB visibility.
items.expected_effect: De-risks ghost and decision-process opacity, builds stakeholder coverage across procurement/legal/IT/finance, and tests whether James is a true Champion (opens doors) or a passive sponsor (blocks access). Multi-threading is required to progress beyond Qualified.

items.action: Run a competitive deep-dive on SAP Ariba. Ask James Noone on the discovery call: is Ariba your incumbent or a shortlist peer, what does Ariba do well and where does it fall short for RWDC, what is Ariba's pricing, and what would Zycus need to prove to win this evaluation. Use the answer to build the wedge (likely Merlin AI + sourcing/contract/supplier integration depth) and position it in the demo.
items.trigger: SAP Ariba is named as competitor but no intelligence on Ariba's role (incumbent vs peer), pricing, buyer sentiment, or wedge exists.
items.expected_effect: Clarifies competitive landscape, surfaces Ariba's weaknesses, and shapes the demo and value case to exploit those gaps. Without competitive intelligence, the demo risks being generic rather than wedge-driven.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 29 May 2026, 37 days old. In Qualified 37 days with 149 days to close (2026-12-01). No qualified_date or stage-date series available so forward velocity cannot be computed from submission milestones. Last verified buyer touch 04 Jun (31 days ago). At 149 days to close, the required pace is a material milestone every 3-4 weeks; 31-day silence without a scheduled next event is a slip contributor. No forward date slips recorded in field history window. Time-in-stage burn rate at this pace would leave ~112 days for discovery, demo, RFI/RFP, shortlist, commercials, and contracting, which is tight but recoverable IF engagement resumes immediately.
evidence: Last buyer activity 04 Jun 2026 per LastActivityDate, 31 days ago.
Single-thread risk: only James Noone (CPO, Decision Maker) is contact-mapped; no EB, Champion, or multi-stakeholder visibility.
No recorded discovery call transcript; 29 May intro call (per Next_Step context) not captured in Avoma, so no buyer-articulated pain, metrics, criteria, or process baseline exists.
SAP Ariba named as competitor in Salesforce Competitor field but no competitive intelligence (incumbent vs peer, buyer sentiment, pricing) available.
Next_Step populated with a detailed 5-point discovery and demo plan but no evidence of buyer confirmation, scheduled date, or two-way commitment to execute it.
Pipeline forecast at $100k ACV with no EB identification, no champion evidence, and 31-day cooling silence is indefensible; recommend Omitted until re-engagement and discovery depth are secured.
north_star_verdict.headline: 31-day buyer silence in early Qualified stage with single-thread exposure, no MEDDPICC depth, and thin discovery baseline.

flags: Single-thread risk: only James Noone (CPO) is mapped. Multi-thread to finance (Economic Buyer), legal, IT, and line-of-business stakeholders during the discovery call to reduce ghost risk and surface decision-process complexity.

Economic Buyer unmapped at $100k ACV. Ask James Noone who controls the budget and commercial approval (likely CFO or senior finance), then map and engage that person before pricing discussions to validate forecast defensibility.

Champion gap: James Noone is Decision Maker but no evidence of advocacy, access, or proactive engagement. During discovery, assess his willingness to introduce the Economic Buyer, co-build the business case, and push internally; if passive, identify an alternative champion with broader reach and executive sponsorship.
Single-thread risk: only James Noone (CPO, Decision Maker) is mapped; no multi-stakeholder visibility, no EB, no Champion, creating ghost and decision-process opacity risk.
31-day buyer silence: last verified activity 04 Jun, no scheduled discovery call or buyer-confirmed next step, deal is cooling.
Economic Buyer not identified: at $100k ACV, budget authority likely sits with CFO or senior finance, but no EB contact role or engagement exists.
Champion gap: James Noone shows no evidence of advocacy, access, or proactive engagement; risk of passive sponsor unable to drive internal momentum.
Thin discovery: 29 May introductory call not recorded, so no buyer-articulated pain, metrics, decision criteria, or process baseline is captured; all MEDDPICC elements are partial or gap.
Competitive intelligence gap: SAP Ariba is named but no detail on Ariba's role (incumbent vs shortlist peer), pricing, or buyer sentiment is available.
best_practice_check.summary: Single-thread risk (only James Noone mapped), no Economic Buyer, no Champion evidence, 31-day buyer silence, thin discovery baseline (29 May call not recorded), and no MEDDPICC depth. Deal is cooling in early Qualified stage with no scheduled next event.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the named competitor but competitive intelligence is a gap. No evidence clarifies whether Ariba is the incumbent (replacement deal) or a shortlist peer (competitive evaluation), what Ariba's pricing or buyer sentiment is, or what wedge Zycus has. Merlin is in scope, which is the natural AI differentiation lever, but no evidence shows it has been positioned or landed with the buyer.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin is in scope per Products field, which signals AI appetite or at least openness, but no evidence shows Merlin has been positioned, demoed, or landed with the buyer. The 29 May call was not recorded, so no conversational evidence of AI needs, Merlin excitement, or negotiation-agent use-case validation exists. Salesforce AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c and AIS fields are unpopulated. The Next_Step references running a tailored demo connecting iSupplier/iSource/iContract but does not explicitly call out Merlin or the agentic narrative. The risk is that Merlin is scoped but not sold. The move is to make Merlin the centerpiece of the discovery demo: position the negotiation agent on supplier price history and contract renewal optimization as the wedge against SAP Ariba.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: No buyer-stated expectations, decision criteria, or evaluation framework have been captured, so fit cannot be assessed. The Next_Step field lists rep-side positioning themes (sourcing event speed, contract renewals, supplier qualification) but these are Zycus's product assumptions, not confirmed buyer priorities. Discovery is required to validate fit.

hard.ais_why:
* As the business scales, RWDC needs "stronger procurement control across sourcing, supplier collaboration, and contract governance" - a foundational control requirement.
* The driver is governance and control across sourcing, SRM and CLM, not AI capability.
* Next step is to "Book discovery with James Noone... and run a tailored demo connecting iSupplier, iSource, and iContract"; Merlin sits in scope but AI is not part of the stated need. Qualified at 10%.
hard.next_step:
• Book discovery with James Noone to confirm RWDC’s priorities across sourcing, contracts, supplier onboarding, and supplier governance.
  • Map current sourcing and supplier categories to show where iSource improves event speed, supplier comparison, savings tracking, and bid control.
  • Review contract workflows with procurement and legal to position iContract around renewals, obligations, approvals, and supplier agreement visibility.
  • Assess supplier onboarding and data quality to align iSupplier with supplier qualification, documentation, profile accuracy, and collaboration.
  • Run a tailored demo and value case connecting iSupplier, iSource, and iContract to procurement scale, supplier reliability, savings discipline, and contract control.
